sentences.text: And--
Right.
That's the tippy top of France.
He made his way down by probably hitchhiking.
But don't forget that France was totally out of gas.
They burned wood.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And there were huge canisters in back of buses and trucks.
Private people had put their cars on blocks.
There-- there wasn't-- there was nothing.
And in the south of France, there was no dairy of any sort-- no butter, no milk, no meat.
We lived by our wits.
It was fascinating, really.
I learned a lot.
The so-called marvelous Provence, the cuisine from Provence, does a lot with sauces.
You just dump a nice, tasty sauce over anything, and you can eat it.
And don't ask questions.
I used to get up once a week, at 4:00 in the morning, and stand in line for horse meat.
And the reason I didn't mind is because my-- my boyfriend was there, too.
So, fine.
Why not?
We got a chance to see each other, and talk, and never mind me being the niece of the boss.
Did your father tell you how he reached you?
OK, my father and mother corresponded.
Although sometimes she didn't hear from him for many weeks.
But he knew we were down there.
That was the only camp in that part of the world.
So it was a given, anyway.
And when he came to claim us, I guess he was very authoritative.
Because they didn't deny him.
And my young friend, the gendarme, said, there's a young man to come-- came to get you.
Now what?
I said, well, you go by yourself.
[LAUGHS] So that was the end of that.
But I had friends that died beside me and around me.
And you grow a thick skin.
You can stand it.
As you live through all this, I think you-- you acquire a shell that you wear.
And otherwise, you wouldn't make it.
Not really that long.
And in that short time-- I think it was somewhere between two to three months-- I saw all that.
And that I've never forgotten.
It was too horrific for words.
So when people talk about gulags and concentration camps, and to have to-- you have to be very strong to put it aside and get on with life.
And I made that decision-- oh, way back.
I was going to have a husband.
I was going to have a family.
I really didn't care where, as long as it wasn't in Europe.
And my father was European.
He didn't want to leave Europe.
He said, I'm too old to make another start.
America is-- is-- is a cultural wasteland.
I don't belong there.
Those were the arguments he had.
Well, what's to say?
It rained every day.
So we sloshed through mud, every day.
It was dismal.
But because I was 18, and because I was female, and because there were an awful lot of men right next door that tried to be nice to me, it wasn't that terrible.
I worked super hard and I ate very little.
And I had to endure watching people die.
I think that is probably the worst memory.
And my mother suffered terribly.
So you get over it, because you have to.
Just my father.
Just from my father.
Yes, I got a lot of correspondence.
I destroyed the letters.
I'm really sorry I did that.
They were interesting letters.
They were written on any kind of scrap of paper.
There was a man from Poland who wrote the-- the piece of music-- it had a name, [GERMAN]-- happiness, family happiness, and he wrote a proposal of marriage on that.
It was so sweet.
He was so desperate.
I always felt bad.
In the camp.
Yes.
I don't know where he got it.
Back to Grasse.
Life resumed-- hard work, no contact with other young people.
I had one friend from Denmark.
Her mother lived in Grasse with her daughter.
And-- Toni Jensen.
She disappeared.
She-- I think they went back to Denmark after the hostilities broke out.
And she didn't stay.
I had no friends, except that one young man that didn't talk to me for a year.
So--
Yeah.
Oh, that's another story.
[LAUGHS] I don't know if I should tell it now.
Because we went back to visit him.
My husband and my children went to visit him.
And when we arrived-- he stayed on that property and managed it.
After my uncle died, my aunt moved away.
Other people lived there, bought this place.
And he managed it.
And I only met his wife, who said, I wish you had married him.
He has never forgotten you.
I never saw him that time.
But we went back again.
And by then, things had deteriorated.
He was mentally deranged.
And he was getting injections for his mental disorder.
And it was sad.
It was a very sad meeting.
Never mind, anyway.
That's another story.
My father's determination to leave became very strong.
And he made many trips to Nice, where the consulate was, the American consulate.
And they were slower than molasses.
We-- should I mention that we harbored quite a few people while we were there?
It-- I remember that very clearly.
People came from Paris mostly, people who either had known my uncle, who was a professor at the Sorbonne, or had heard about him.
And they would come and stay with us.
We'd give them shelter, advice, food.
And they would go on to get away from France.
And I kind of felt very good about that.
By then, I had a-- I was getting older.
And I knew exactly what was going on.
And I was very interested in my own life.
Anyway, my father and I made one last, desperate trip to Nice.
And I'll never forget that.
Because on the way, there was-- our bus had an accident.
And I know one young child was killed in this accident.
And what I remember so vividly was a couple sitting by the roads.
And she was saying to her husband, who was in tears, don't worry.
I'll make you another child.
It was so touching.
They were peasants.
And I've never forgotten it.
Because it's simplicity itself.
Don't worry.
I'll make you another one.
Sweet, very sweet.
The American consul finally gave us a visa, on his suitcases.
And so after Pearl Harbor, we left.
That was December '41.
Well, he was leaving.
America had endured Pearl Harbor.
And they finally woke up.
They were closing down.
Yeah.
Well, the place next door, for instance-- these were all gorgeous villas with acreage.
They're not really farms, you know?
They are gentleman ranches type thing.
The one-- the army requisitioned them.
So way back, there were soldiers stationed there from Africa.
And I remember distinctly feeling terrible for these poor guys.
I used to come and visit and speak to them.
They were homesick.
They were not eating the French food, because that wasn't what they were used to.
They were from tribes in Africa-- gorgeous young men that were surely going to be killed and-- because they were gun fodder.
That's it.
These were French soldiers.
And they were wearing red fezzes that made them-- distinguished them from the rest of the military.
But of course, after armistice, when I came back from camp, that was all gone.
The people around me were anti-German.
So my-- naturally, my aunt tried to hide me as well as possible, to my chagrin.
I don't think so.
My friend [?
Toni ?] did.
And of course, my boyfriend did.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think so.
We were German.
Like in this country, they killed German Shepherd dogs.
Why?
Because they were German dogs.
We--
They knew we were refugees.
So most people don't go any further.
They know you're hiding out.
They don't really ask what religion you are.
It doesn't come up.
You just try to stay out of trouble.
And food was of primary importance.
We did not have time to think of anything else.
We were all starving, really.
We prepared meals made of don't ask what.
And I think one big, terrible thing that I remember-- my aunt screamed hysterically.
She had saved a sack of flour.
And because she was so careful with it, and didn't use it, it got bugs in it.
And so she spread the flour out on the table outside.
And it took-- took to the air, literally.
They were very hard times-- not easy.
And the land did not really feed us.
This was for market.
But one wonderful thing happened.
And it has nothing to do with anything.
My father taught-- my uncle taught Sanskrit at the Sorbonne.
Sanskrit?
So he had some very odd friends that came to visit.
One gentleman came through.
He was an archaeologist.
And he had returned from Egypt.
And he had dug up a pharaoh's tomb and found a sheaf of wheat.
And he gave it to my uncle and he said, you must plant it.
It will germinate.
It will grow.
So it was planted by the light of the moon, thrown over the left shoulder of the person planting it.
And it germinated, grew [? men's-- ?] over men's tall.
It made all the papers.
It was unbelievable.
I was just thrilled.
Unbelievable.
And I remember being delighted by very simple things.
France-- that part of France has the same climate that the desert in-- in California has, really.
We had tanks for water.
And we kept water in the bathtubs.
We had droughts.
And it was wonderful.
When it rained, I ran outside in my bathing suit and just let it rain on me.
You know, I was young.
And I came out of all this much better than my parents.
They were quite beaten down.
How did your boyfriend treat you?
My boyfriend?
He was just thrilled.
I was the niece of the boss.
I mean, that's all.
You know, no religious-- he was Catholic.
It's a Catholic country.
But I became jaded very quickly.
Because across the street was a girl that I used to like to talk to.
And she disappeared all of a sudden.
And it turns out that she was impregnated by Monsieur le [?
Coure-- ?] that is, the young village priest.
She showed up and had lost a lot of weight.
She came back from her aunt, or whatever.
And it was an open secret-- things like that.
In France we were very careful, because I was German-- very careful.
I wasn't allowed any social life.
I literally was a prisoner, really, if you want to call a spade a spade.
And so I cherished the few friendships that I had.
But I felt that they were three lost years-- just about three years.
And I had to make up for lost time, when I came to New York.
And I did, [LAUGHS] big time.
Oh yes, oh yes, especially since we were helping people all the time.
That was very dangerous.
Just listening to the radio was dangerous.
But for the-- for the most part, you know, people are people, wherever you go.
You-- when-- when a simple person wants to show their affection for you, or their loyalty, or whatever, friendship, we did it with food.
If-- if I was able to get a little extra sugar-- we didn't have any sugar.
The French were so badly prepared for this war that there was nothing, except maybe what grew on the land.
You would share it.
You would share it with those near you.
I remember doing that.
Oh, [FRENCH], the police.
Not much.
At the end, when I needed the-- it's called the [FRENCH], that lets you out of France.
I was the one they sent.
Because my father was just too shaken.
And my mother wouldn't have dreamed of doing that.
But I had no more qualm about anything.
I was pretty brave in those days.
I went to the city.
And I went to city hall.
I almost broke my foot up on the steps and had to have some help to get home.
But I remember picking up all the papers.
Out of here.
And then they were trying to convince me to stay.
You speak such beautiful French.
Why don't you stay?
You're more French than German.
Don't go.
Those were kind people.
They didn't mean any harm.
But unlike a lot of other people in France, I got away with not being pers-- persecuted for being Jewish.
I realize that.
Who didn't want you to go?
Oh, just plain, nice people in town who said, no don't, don't.
Don't leave your boyfriend.
You know, so it was very normal, really.
I don't have any story about being hidden by French people, or-- or being-- I knew about those things, though.
We knew it was all around us.
And we were very fortunate to get away.
No one thought we would make it, by the way.
I need to tell you that.
They all said good bye forever.
Well, the people that we sheltered knew.
About being persecuted, or being-- people disappearing.
Vichy government was incredibly corrupt and totally in-- in the German's pocket.
That was no secret.
We all knew that.
It was dangerous.
Not then.
Because the Germans occupied-- they covered all of France, eventually.
And we-- when we left from Marseilles, the Germans were coming in the north end of the city-- very uncomfortable.
Very close.
It was loaded with Jews, of course.
That was the only camp in that region.
The one in Cap d'Antibes was for men only.
And it was beginning of the war.
And they took people into the army from there.
But this-- this is where you rotted and died.
Not a pleasant place.
OK, by train to Marseilles.
And my poor parents had all the chores to do.
We secured passage on a ship that went to Algiers.
Yes.
I had an exit permit, a [FRENCH].
We all did.
This is-- these are the papers I picked up.
And so we got to Algiers.
And people slept all over that ship, on deck.
I don't-- it was incredible.
And we had meals, as if it was a luxury liner, with first seating and second seating, OK?
So at our table, I met a gentleman who said, I want you to visit me when you come to Casablanca.
And I did.
Well, I remember incredible fear, paranoia-- here we go again type feeling.
You know, we just tried to get away from that in Germany.
And here it was again.
It was horrible for my parents.
I was such an optimist, and so young and daring at that time, and glad to be amongst people where I could talk to anybody I wanted to for a change.
I lived a very confined life for almost three years.
We took care of each other.
You know, if I could get something for them, I would do that.
And I don't know how well they would have done without me.
They were worn out.
Not terribly well, no.
Well, it's-- it's understandable, too.
Because here we are.
We-- whole family moved in on them.
You know, but I didn't have that kind of understanding.
I was young and demanding.
You give me shelter.
I remember bringing all this money to you.
So it's your turn-- that attitude.
Well, we made several trips-- vacation trips.
And I remember sitting on the money.
It was a-- usually in a box of some sort, in our car.
And at the borders, they searched our cars.
And the dumb thing is that we went-- came back to Germany.
I mean, how stupid can you get?
In retrospect.
This was in 1938, in the summer, in '37.
I mean, what was he doing?
What was he thinking?
But hindsight is 20/20 vision.
Yes, there was a cousin.
And I hated her because she was going to school.
And she had friends.
After we left, they had a much worse time, too.
They had to hide.
I know that.
This was after Pearl Harbor.
So it was 1942.
And the Germans had occupied all of France.
They built shelters.
See, before I left, this-- there was no occupation yet.
I got away with it.
While we were living there?
No.
We lived on my mother's jewelry, which my dad sold in Nice.
And my-- I think there was enough money, yes.
There was nothing to buy.
What could you buy?
Not even food.
It was a hard time.
Partisan groups-- no, not really.
I think we just did our own thing.
We helped people who asked for it.
And it was dangerous enough, especially if you're not skilled in martial warfare [LAUGHS] or whatever.
I know I've-- I know a lot about what happened elsewhere.
But I didn't know it then.
I-- I learned about it later, what was going on.
So I felt very fortunate to get away.
Yes, there were other people my parents' age mostly, living all around.
There were beautiful little towns in that area.
And they all thought they were safe.
They immigrated to those places.
And they thought that was it.
But that was not true.
And I think my parents lost track of them.
They lost track of each other.
And many of them probably did not survive because of what happened later-- treachery and all kinds of things.
Well, vividly.
My father finally said he couldn't take it anymore.
It was late.
But it was better than never-- much better.
OK, that-- the ship.
We got off and we got on an electric train.
I remember it was an electric train to Casablanca.
It was a long ride.
And I never sat down.
Because there were no seats.
And there were people all over this train-- on top of the train, under the train, and in the hallway, in the walkways.
But again-- I'm sorry to tell you-- I had a great time.
Because I was so hungry for human intercourse that it was fun.
And someone explained the stol-- stars to me.
And you know, it was just neat.
And we got to Casablanca.
And that was horrific.
Because we had a room.
But there was-- everybody wanted to get out.
And the movie is about that.
And that's what was going on, all these desperate people trying to get away.
And Morocco was French [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time.
But I had been given the card by this gentleman, whom I call le Monsieur [?
Cris. ?] And he said, come visit me when you come to Casablanca.
So I did.
And he had kids my age.
And they said, we don't care.
You're a new girl in town.
We're so happy to see you.
Come, we'll show you the city.
It was a very interesting city.
It was pretty badly damaged later, during the war, like Rabat and Dakar, and all these places were bombed.
The Germans were there.
But there was a window during that time.
And we went to the kasbahs.
There were two kasbahs.
And one was allowed.
One wasn't.
So I had lived the life of [?
Riley. ?] I remember buying oranges in a horse drawn buggy and having no-- no problems, believe me.
And I never drank a lot of alcohol.
I didn't need it.
I was naturally effervescent.
I had a great time with those people and frankly-- unfortunately, did not keep in touch.
Because what came later was totally difficult.
Like when you have no food, that's all you think of.
And when you come to a new country, you can only think of how do I support myself?
So I lost track of them.
But that's OK.
It was incredible.
And where did you meet this man?
On the ship from Marseilles to Algiers.
He was very elegant.
And he was not a refugee.
He just happened to be going back to Casablanca.
He had a crystal and silver store-- a splendid store.
And I guess he wanted to have his children enjoy somebody new.
Because the-- the Jewish community in Casablanca was narrow, not big.
Two weeks, two horrific weeks.
For me it was fun, but not for my parents.
Because they couldn't get a ship to get away.
My dad tried to-- I think we're supposed to stop, no?
